points.text: The US government froze assets of the Issei and imposed strict curfews after Pearl Harbor.
The FBI followed community leaders with strong Japanese ties and raided homes for contraband.
Japanese Americans were detained and interned as a result of official policy during WWII.
Interned Japanese Americans could only take what they could carry, sustaining them for years.
Anti-Japanese sentiment and aggression were widespread after Pearl Harbor, supporting removal policies.
The Vietnam War inspired widespread dissent within the United States due to military intervention in support of a corrupt regime.
American support for a military coup in Chile led to organized terror against opposition, causing international outrage.
Kissinger and Cold War intellectuals limited democracy at home by not subjecting their policies to public debate.
Kissinger's Realpolitik focused on security over human rights, despite domestic protests advocating for the latter.
Nixon and Kissinger aimed to preserve American power credibility by mixing de-escalation with force in Vietnam.
Senator Joe McCarthy used war hysteria to assault civil liberties in the U.S.
The Espionage Act of 1917 criminalized obstructing military recruitment and aiding the enemy.
The Sedition Act of 1918 prohibited disloyal language against the government and armed forces.
The Supreme Court upheld the Espionage Act, allowing speech curtailment during wartime.
The Committee on Public Information controlled war-related information to shape public opinion.
The federal government centralized and coordinated the economy for total war mobilization.
